Contents
CLAUDE.md
Project Overview
Build Commands
Architecture
Adding New Content


CLAUDE.md
This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
Project Overview
This is the course website for EDS 231: Text and Sentiment Analysis for Environmental Problems, a MEDS (Masters of Environmental Data Science) course at UC Santa Barbara’s Bren School. It is built with Distill for R Markdown.
Build Commands
All building is done through R/RStudio. There is no Makefile or CLI build script.
Render the full site (from R console):
rmarkdown::render_site()
Render a single page:
rmarkdown::render("week-2.Rmd")
Output goes to docs/ (configured in _site.yml as output_dir: docs). The site is served via GitHub Pages from that directory.
Architecture
Framework: distill::distill_website — each .Rmd in the root renders to a page in docs/
Navigation: defined in _site.yml (navbar links, site name, GitHub link)
Theme: theme.css — UCSB branding (navy #003660 titles, teal headers, Sanchez/Nunito Sans/Roboto Mono fonts)
Footer: _footer.html — Bren School logo and attribution
Content structure:
- index.Rmd — home/course overview page
- week-N.Rmd — weekly lecture/content pages (use week-template.Rmd as a starting point)
- Labs/Lab{N}.Rmd — student-facing lab assignments
- Labs/key-Lab{N}.Rmd — answer keys (committed; Keys/ directory is gitignored for draft keys)
- data/ — sample datasets (e.g., NYT article CSVs)
- Nexis/ — Nexis Uni raw data files
Key R packages used in labs:
- tidytext — core text analysis (tokenization, tf-idf, sentiment)
- tidyverse / ggplot2 — wrangling and visualization
- jsonlite — NYT API responses
- LexisNexisTools — Nexis Uni data ingestion
Adding New Content
New weekly pages: copy week-template.Rmd, name it week-N.Rmd, then add it to the navbar in _site.yml
The Keys/ directory is gitignored — place draft answer keys there to keep them out of version control
API keys (e.g., NYT API) should never be committed; students set them locally via Sys.setenv()

Contents
Course description
Instructor
Important links
Weekly course schedule
Learning objectives
Course requirements
Computing
Textbook

Topics




Figure 1: Text and sentiment analysis for environmental problems



Course description
This course will cover foundations and applications of natural language processing. Problem sets and class projects will leverage common and emerging text-based data sources relevant to environmental problems, including policy documents, public comments, scientific literature, and social media. Methods covered include text processing, sentiment analysis, text classification and topic modeling.
Instructor
Mateo Robbins (mjrobbins@ucsb.edu)
Office: Bren Hall 2027
Office hours: Tuesdays, by appointment
Important links
Link to full course syllabus
Weekly course schedule
Class meets: Bren 1510
Tuesdays 12:30pm -1:45pm    
Learning objectives
The goal of EDS 231 (Text and Sentiment Analysis for Environmental Problems) is to expose students to a range of text and sentiment analysis data sources, techniques and tools that can be applied to environmental problems. During this course, students will:
Become familiar with the common software packages used in text-as-data applications 
Conduct and explain each step in the text data collection, analysis, and presentation pipeline 
Evaluate examples of text analysis in the environmental science literature
Course requirements
Computing
Minimum MEDS device requirements
Up-to-date R and RStudio
Textbook
Readings will be drawn from free online ebooks:
Text Mining with R (TMR)
Supervised Machine Learning for Text Analysis in R (SMLTAR)

Topics
Week
Topic
1
Course Intro
2
Text Preprocessing and Cleaning
3
Exploratory Text Analysis and Visualization
4
Text Representation I
5
Text Representation II
6
Sentiment Analysis I
7
Sentiment Analysis II
8
Classification
9
TBD
10
Group presentations

Lab 2: Text Analysis of Nexis Uni Data
Data Center News Coverage
2026-04-14





Overview
In this lab you will work with a corpus of news articles about data
centers. They were written in the first 6 months of either 2021 or 2025
and retrieved from Nexis Uni and processed with the
LexisNexisTools package. You will practice:
Loading and inspecting a structured news corpus
Tokenization, stemming, and lemmatization
Building and applying domain-specific stopword lists
Frequency analysis and visualization
Keyness analysis to compare sub-corpora
Packages used:
library(tidyverse)      # data wrangling and plotting
library(tidytext)       # text mining in tidy format
#library(LexisNexisTools) # reading Nexis Uni .docx exports
library(SnowballC)      # Porter stemmer
library(textstem)       # lemmatization
library(scales)         # axis formatting
library(lubridate)      # date handling
library(quanteda)       # corpus and DFM construction
library(quanteda.textstats) # textstat_keyness()
library(quanteda.textplots) # textplot_keyness()


1. Load and Inspect the Corpus
Today’s example uses data from Nexis Uni (formerly LexisNexis), a
news and full-text publications database accessible through the UCSB
library. We’ll examine media coverage of data center construction.
I used the {LexisNexisTools} package to parse
.docx exports from Nexis. It returns an “S4” object with
three slots accessed via @:
@meta — article-level metadata (date, headline,
newspaper)
@articles — full article text
@paragraphs — paragraph-level text
# pre_files <- list.files(
#   path = here::here("Nexis", "data-center"),
#   pattern = "\\.docx$",
#   full.names = TRUE,
#   recursive = TRUE,
#   ignore.case = TRUE
# )
# 
# pre_dat <- lnt_read(pre_files)
# 
# pre_meta_df       <- pre_dat@meta
# pre_articles_df   <- pre_dat@articles
# pre_paragraphs_df <- pre_dat@paragraphs
# 
# df <- tibble(
#   date     = pre_meta_df$Date,
#   headline = pre_meta_df$Headline,
#   id       = pre_meta_df$ID,
#   text     = pre_articles_df$Article,
#   source   = pre_meta_df$Newspaper
# )

url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MaRo406/EDS-231-text-sentiment/refs/heads/main/data/nexis-dat.csv"
df <- read_csv(url)

1.1 Cleaning the raw text
Before any analysis, the raw Nexis export needs cleaning. Each step
below addresses a specific artifact of how Nexis packages its data:
# Drop articles with no date — these are often metadata rows, not real articles
df <- df[!is.na(df$date), ]

# Remove URLs — Nexis embeds source URLs inline in the text body
df$text <- gsub("https?\\S+", "", df$text)

# Convert to ASCII — Nexis exports sometimes include curly quotes, em-dashes,
# and other Unicode characters that cause issues in tokenization
df$text <- iconv(df$text, to = "ASCII//TRANSLIT", sub = "")

# Also, remove possessives
df$text <- gsub("'s\\b", "", df$text, ignore.case = TRUE)
Always good to do a quick inspection:
glimpse(df)
## Rows: 558
## Columns: 6
## $ ...1     [3m[38;5;246m<dbl>[39m[23m 1[38;5;246m, [39m2[38;5;246m, [39m3[38;5;246m, [39m4[38;5;246m, [39m5[38;5;246m, [39m6[38;5;246m, [39m7[38;5;246m, [39m8[38;5;246m, [39m9[38;5;246m, [39m10[38;5;246m, [39m11[38;5;246m, [39m12[38;5;246m, [39m13[38;5;246m, [39m14[38;5;246m, [39m15[38;5;246m, [39m16[38;5;246m, [39m17[38;5;246m, [39m18[38;5;246m, [39m19[38;5;246m, [39m20[38;5;246m, [39m21[38;5;246m, [39m22[38;5;246m, [39m23[38;5;246m, [39m24[38;5;246m, [39m25[38;5;246m, [39m26[38;5;246m, [39m27[38;5;246m, [39m28[38;5;246m, [39m29[38;5;246m, [39m30[38;5;246m, [39m31[38;5;246m, [39m32[38;5;246m, [39m33[38;5;246m, [39m34[38;5;246m, [39m35[38;5;246m, [39m36[38;5;246m, [39m37…
## $ date     [3m[38;5;246m<date>[39m[23m 2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2021-05-23[38;5;246m, [39m2021-03-09[38;5;246m, [39m2025-06-26[38;5;246m, [39m2025-02-20[38;5;246m, [39m2025-01-20[38;5;246m, [39m2025-03-05[38;5;246m, [39m2025-03-28[38;5;246m, [39m2025-01-12[38;5;246m, [39m2025-01-19[38;5;246m, [39m2025-04-09[38;5;246m, [39m2025…
## $ headline [3m[38;5;246m<chr>[39m[23m "\"Between the Storms\" chronicles a biologist's adventures on St. John"[38;5;246m, [39m"\"China proved it will be a key ally for Argentina's recovery\…
## $ id       [3m[38;5;246m<dbl>[39m[23m 1[38;5;246m, [39m2[38;5;246m, [39m3[38;5;246m, [39m4[38;5;246m, [39m5[38;5;246m, [39m6[38;5;246m, [39m7[38;5;246m, [39m8[38;5;246m, [39m9[38;5;246m, [39m10[38;5;246m, [39m11[38;5;246m, [39m12[38;5;246m, [39m13[38;5;246m, [39m14[38;5;246m, [39m15[38;5;246m, [39m16[38;5;246m, [39m17[38;5;246m, [39m18[38;5;246m, [39m19[38;5;246m, [39m20[38;5;246m, [39m21[38;5;246m, [39m22[38;5;246m, [39m23[38;5;246m, [39m24[38;5;246m, [39m25[38;5;246m, [39m26[38;5;246m, [39m27[38;5;246m, [39m28[38;5;246m, [39m29[38;5;246m, [39m30[38;5;246m, [39m31[38;5;246m, [39m32[38;5;246m, [39m33[38;5;246m, [39m34[38;5;246m, [39m35[38;5;246m, [39m36[38;5;246m, [39m37…
## $ text     [3m[38;5;246m<chr>[39m[23m "Biologist, cartographer and builder Barry Devine has published a memoir, \"Between the Storms,\" chronicling his adventures on St  John …
## $ source   [3m[38;5;246m<chr>[39m[23m "The Virgin Islands Daily News, St. Thomas"[38;5;246m, [39m"CE Noticias Financieras English"[38;5;246m, [39m"The Daily Texan: University of Texas - Austin"[38;5;246m, [39m"The Dai…
cat("\nDate range:", format(min(df$date)), "to", format(max(df$date)), "\n")
## 
## Date range: 2021-01-01 to 2025-06-30
cat("Unique sources:", n_distinct(df$source), "\n")
## Unique sources: 136
cat("Total articles:", nrow(df), "\n")
## Total articles: 558
df |>
  count(source, sort = TRUE) |>
  filter(n > 10, source != "") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(source, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7bb6") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Articles by Source",
       x = NULL, y = "Article count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)




2. Tokenization
The core tidytext workflow converts documents into a
one-token-per-row format using
unnest_tokens(). This makes every subsequent step a
standard dplyr pipeline ##— you can filter, join, and count
tokens using the same verbs you already know.##
tokens <- df |>
  select(id, date, source, text) |> #basically just leaving out headline here
  unnest_tokens(
    output = word,    # name of the new token column
    input  = text,    # column containing text
    token  = "words"  # tokenize by word (also: "sentences", "ngrams")
  )

glimpse(tokens)
## Rows: 634,605
## Columns: 4
## $ id     [3m[38;5;246m<dbl>[39m[23m 1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1[38;5;246m, [39m1…
## $ date   [3m[38;5;246m<date>[39m[23m 2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-05-01[38;5;246m, [39m2025-0…
## $ source [3m[38;5;246m<chr>[39m[23m "The Virgin Islands Daily News, St. Thomas"[38;5;246m, [39m"The Virgin Islands Daily News, St. Thomas"[38;5;246m, [39m"The Virgin Islands Daily News, St. Thomas"[38;5;246m, [39m"The…
## $ word   [3m[38;5;246m<chr>[39m[23m "biologist"[38;5;246m, [39m"cartographer"[38;5;246m, [39m"and"[38;5;246m, [39m"builder"[38;5;246m, [39m"barry"[38;5;246m, [39m"devine"[38;5;246m, [39m"has"[38;5;246m, [39m"published"[38;5;246m, [39m"a"[38;5;246m, [39m"memoir"[38;5;246m, [39m"between"[38;5;246m, [39m"the"[38;5;246m, [39m"storms"[38;5;246m, [39m"chronicli…
cat("\nTotal tokens:", nrow(tokens), "\n")
## 
## Total tokens: 634605
cat("Unique tokens:", n_distinct(tokens$word), "\n")
## Unique tokens: 24396
Discussion: What does unnest_tokens()
do to case and punctuation by default?



3. Stopword Removal

3.1 Standard Stopwords
Recall that tidytext ships with several stopword
lexicons via get_stopwords(). The most comprehensive
built-in option is the SMART list, which covers around 570
high-frequency function words and is well-suited for news and document
analysis.
standard_sw <- get_stopwords(source = "smart", )

tokens_clean <- tokens |>
  anti_join(standard_sw, by = "word")

cat("Tokens after standard stopword removal:", nrow(tokens_clean), "\n")
## Tokens after standard stopword removal: 325237


3.2 Domain-Specific Stopwords
Standard lists were built for general text. News about data centers
will include high-frequency words that are uninformative for
your research questions — boilerplate legal language, news wire
attributions, and domain-generic terms.

Methodological note: Your stopword choices are
analytical decisions, not neutral preprocessing. Document them
and be prepared to justify them. A word that is uninformative for one
research question may be the key signal for another.

Step 1: Look at what’s still most frequent after standard
stopword removal.
tokens_clean |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 30) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(word, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#d7191c") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 30 Words (after standard stopwords)",
       x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Step 2: Build a domain stopword list from what you
observe.
Look at the plot above. Add words that are frequent but analytically
uninformative for your research question. Can you identify anthing that
we should remove?



domain_sw <- tibble(word = c(
  # News wire / attribution boilerplate
  "said", "says", "told", "according", "reported", "noted", "added",
  "stated", "announced", "mr", "ms", "inc", "corp", "llc",
  # Generic quantitative terms ##(adjust based on your research question)
  "year", "years", "percent", "million", "billion", "quarter",
  "company", "companies", "firm", "business",
  # Consider whether these are substantive for your RQ:
  "data", "center", "centers"  # usually want to analyze words around the key terms, not the terms themselves
))

tokens_clean2 <- tokens_clean |>
  anti_join(domain_sw, by = "word") |>
  filter(
    !str_detect(word, "^[0-9]+$"),  # remove pure numbers, ^ is start string, $ is end, [0-9]+ is one or more digits ([0-9] is character class meaning digit, + means 1 or more)
    str_length(word) > 2            # remove very short tokens
  )

cat("Tokens after domain stopword removal:", nrow(tokens_clean2), "\n")
## Tokens after domain stopword removal: 296257
cat("Reduction from raw:", scales::percent(1 - nrow(tokens_clean2) / nrow(tokens)), "\n") #little trick to turn this into a string
## Reduction from raw: 53%
Exercise: Inspect the frequency plot again after
domain stopword removal. Are there additional terms you would add? What
is your criterion for inclusion?



4. Stemming and Lemmatization
Both stemming and lemmatization reduce words to a base form to
consolidate variant spellings. They differ in approach and output.

4.1 Stemming (Porter Stemmer)
tokens_stemmed <- tokens_clean2 |>
  mutate(stem = wordStem(word, language = "english"))

# Compare original tokens to their stems
tokens_stemmed |>
  filter(word != stem) |>
  distinct(word, stem) |>
  slice_head(n = 20) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Sample: original tokens vs. stems")
Sample: original tokens vs. stems
word
stem
cartographer
cartograph
barry
barri
devine
devin
published
publish
storms
storm
chronicling

adventures

story
stori
finding
find
loving
love
leaving
leav
paradise
paradis
early
earli
realized
realiz
questions
question
parents
parent
lives
live
unanswered
unansw
dreams
dream
successes
success
Look at the stem column. Do any stems look wrong or ambiguous?


4.2 Lemmatization
tokens_lemmatized <- tokens_clean2 |>
  mutate(lemma = lemmatize_words(word))

# Compare
tokens_lemmatized |>
  filter(word != lemma) |>
  distinct(word, lemma) |>
  slice_head(n = 20) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Sample: original tokens vs. lemmas")
Sample: original tokens vs. lemmas
word
lemma
published
publish
storms
storm
chronicling
chronicle
adventures
adventure
finding
find
loving
love
leaving
leave
older
old
realized
realize
questions
question
parents
parent
lives
life
left
leave
dreams
dream
successes
success
inspired
inspire
answers
answer
thought
think
born
bear
queens
queen
Compare the two approaches on a specific word
family:
comparison_words <- c("operating", "operated", "operations", "operator", "operators",
                      "building", "built", "builds", "build")

tokens_clean2 |>
  filter(word %in% comparison_words) |>
  mutate(
    stem  = wordStem(word),
    lemma = lemmatize_words(word)
  ) |>
  distinct(word, stem, lemma) |>
  arrange(word) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Stemming vs. lemmatization comparison")
Stemming vs. lemmatization comparison
word
stem
lemma
build
build
build
building
build
build
builds
build
build
built
built
build
operated
oper
operate
operating
oper
operate
operations
oper
operation
operator
oper
operator
operators
oper
operator
Question: Which approach would you choose for this
corpus, and why? Consider your downstream analysis tasks — frequency
counts benefit from lemmatization (real words are easier to interpret),
while keyness analysis is less sensitive to the choice since both
corpora are treated the same way.
For the rest of this lab we will use lemmatized
tokens.
working_tokens <- tokens_lemmatized |>
  mutate(word = lemma) |>
  select(-lemma)



5. Frequency Analysis

5.1 Overall Frequency
top_words <- working_tokens |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 30)

top_words |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(word, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1a9641") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "30 Most Frequent Terms — Data Center Corpus",
    subtitle = "After stopword removal and lemmatization",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)



5.2 Frequency by Source
Raw frequency across the whole corpus can mask differences between
outlets. This plot shows each source’s top 10 terms independently —
useful for spotting whether specific publications drive particular terms
in the overall frequency list.
top_sources <- df |>
  count(source, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 10) |> #select rows with largest values
  pull(source) #extract source column only

working_tokens |>
  filter(source %in% top_sources) |>
  count(source, word, sort = TRUE) |> #ungroup,transform,group allows calculations but maintain row structure
  group_by(source) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder_within(word, n, source), y = n, fill = source)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~source, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Terms by Source", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)




6. Keyness Analysis
Frequency tells us which words are common. Keyness tells us which
words are characteristic — more common in one sub-corpus than
you would expect given their overall rate. A word can be infrequent
overall but highly distinctive of a particular group.
We use quanteda for this because tidytext
doesn’t implement keyness statistics natively. quanteda
represents text as a document-feature matrix (DFM) —
the wide-format equivalent of the long tidy token table we’ve been
using.

6.1 Build a corpus and DFM
# Build corpus — quanteda uses character doc IDs, so id (integer) is coerced
corp <- corpus(df, docid_field = "id", text_field = "text") #Where our tidy tokens had one row per token, a DFM has one row per document and one column per unique token, with cell values being token counts.

# Create a new document variable (year) from date
docvars(corp, "year") <- format(df$date, "%Y")

# Tokenize inside quanteda: remove punctuation, numbers, symbols with (remove_*); lowercase 
toks <- tokens(corp,
               remove_punct   = TRUE,
               remove_numbers = TRUE,
               remove_symbols = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en", source = "smart") ) #

# Build DFM and drop any documents with no date
dfmat <- dfm(toks)
dfmat <- dfmat[!is.na(docvars(dfmat, "year")), ]

cat("DFM dimensions:", nrow(dfmat), "documents ×", ncol(dfmat), "features\n")
## DFM dimensions: 558 documents × 25603 features


6.2 Define target and reference sub-corpora
We compare 2021 coverage against 2025 coverage. This span captures
the period just before and after large-scale AI infrastructure
investment became a dominant news frame — the launch of ChatGPT (late
2022) and subsequent hyperscaler announcements sit in between. You could
equally split by source, geographic region, or any other variable in
docvars.
cat("2021 articles:", sum(docvars(dfmat, "year") == "2021"), "\n")
## 2021 articles: 246
cat("2025 articles:", sum(docvars(dfmat, "year") == "2025"), "\n")
## 2025 articles: 312
# Collapse DFM to two rows — one per year value
dfmat_grouped <- dfm_group(dfmat, groups = year)


6.3 Compute and plot keyness
textstat_keyness() defaults to
measure = "chi2". We use measure = "lr"
(log-likelihood ratio, G²) because it handles sparse counts better than
χ².
result <- textstat_keyness(dfmat_grouped,
                           target  = "2025",
                           measure = "lr")

textplot_keyness(result, n = 20) +
  labs(
    title = "Keyness: Data Center Coverage",
    subtitle = "Target: 2025  |  Reference: 2021  |  Statistic: Log-Likelihood (G²)"
  )

Create a new .Rmd file for your submission. Use the lab code as a
reference, but your submission should contain only your own analysis —
not the demo walkthrough. For the submission, answer each of the
questions in part 7 and part 8 below.



7. Interpretive questions:
What do the most positive keywords (overrepresented in 2025) tell
you about how data center coverage has shifted?
Your answer here
Are any of these keywords surprising? What might explain their
appearance?
Your answer here
Keyness flags statistical unusualness, not substantive
importance. A high-G² word could be driven by a single prolific source
or a short news spike. How would you check whether a keyword is
genuinely distributed across the corpus rather than concentrated in one
place?
Your answer here
How has the language used to describe data centers in news
coverage changed over time, and what does that suggest about the
evolving journalistic frame around the issue?
Your answer here


8. Exercises
Stopword sensitivity: Remove all
domain-specific stopwords and re-run the frequency plot. Then add 10
more domain stopwords beyond what was provided. How do your results
change? What does this tell you about the analytical choices embedded in
preprocessing?
Your answer here
Alternative keyness comparison: Instead of
comparing time periods, compare the two sources with the most articles.
What does keyness reveal about how each publication frames the data
center beat?
Your answer here
Bigrams: Use
unnest_tokens(token = "ngrams", n = 2) to extract bigrams.
What are the 20 most frequent bigrams? Do they reveal entities or
conceptual phrases that single-token analysis misses?
Your answer here

Week 2: NYT API
EDS 231 — Spring 2026
April 14, 2026





An introduction to text preprocessing and the New York Times article
API.


Learning Objectives
By the end of this week, you will be able to:
Make API calls in R — constructing URLs with query parameters and
authenticating with an API key
Conduct exploratory data analysis — visualizing temporal patterns in
media coverage using ggplot2
Apply string manipulation — cleaning strings using functions like
str* and gsub()
Use stop word dictionaries — removing common uninformative words
from text data to prepare it for analysis


Recommended Reading
📖 TMR
1.0-1.3, SMLTR

Week 3: Nexis Uni
EDS 231 — Spring 2026
April 7, 2026





Nexis Uni data.


Learning Objectives
By the end of this week, you will be able to: –>
Clean raw corpus text — removing URLs, encoding artifacts, and
source-specific boilerplate
Build domain-specific stopword lists — identifying and removing
corpus-specific uninformative terms beyond standard lexicons
Apply stemming and lemmatization — reducing tokens to base forms
using the Porter stemmer and dictionary-based lemmatization, and
evaluate the tradeoffs between them
Conduct frequency analysis — counting and visualizing term frequency
across the full corpus and by source
Perform keyness analysis — comparing word use across sub-corpora
using log-likelihood (G²) with quanteda to identify characteristic
terms


Recommended Reading
📖 SMLTR Ch.4

to Canvas.

Getting oriented: what we’ll cover, how we’ll work, and why it
matters.


Overview
This week we set the foundation for the course. We’ll introduce the
core concepts, review the syllabus, and get hands-on with the tools
we’ll use throughout the semester. By the end of this week you should
have a clear sense of where we’re going and how to succeed.

Learning Objectives
By the end of this week, you will be able to:
Describe the course arc and major themes
Set up your computing environment and verify it works
Explain the core vocabulary introduced in lecture
Complete and submit the first assignment
